title: Chapter 5 Quiz: Gatekeepers
description: Test your mastery of users, groups, ownership, and Linux permissions.

questions.question: Which command displays your current username?
questions.explanation: `whoami` prints the effective username.

questions.question: The file `/etc/passwd` stores user password hashes.
questions.explanation: False. Password hashes are in `/etc/shadow`. `/etc/passwd` is world-readable and contains only an 'x' placeholder.

questions.question: What is the UID of the root user?
questions.explanation: Root always has UID 0.

questions.question: The file that defines all system groups is `/etc/_____`.
questions.explanation: `/etc/group` contains group definitions and membership lists.

questions.question: Which command changes the group ownership of a file to 'developers'?
questions.explanation: `chgrp` changes group ownership.

questions.question: A regular user can use `chown` to change the owner of their own file to another user.
questions.explanation: False. Only root can use `chown` to change file ownership.

questions.question: In the permission string `-rwxr-x---`, what can the Group do?
questions.explanation: Group triad is `r-x`: Read and Execute.

questions.question: What does the first character in `drwxr-xr-x` represent?
questions.explanation: `d` stands for directory.

questions.question: What numeric (octal) value corresponds to `rw-`?
questions.explanation: Read (4) + Write (2) = 6.

questions.question: Which `chmod` command gives the owner read/write/execute and everyone else read-only?
questions.explanation: 744 = Owner rwx (7), Group r-- (4), Others r-- (4).

questions.question: Which symbolic `chmod` command removes write permission for others?
questions.explanation: `o-w` means 'others minus write'.

questions.question: You have write permission on a directory but not on a file inside it. Can you delete the file?
questions.explanation: Yes. Deleting a file requires write permission on the parent directory, not on the file itself.

questions.question: Having execute permission on a directory allows you to `cd` into it.
questions.explanation: True. Execute on a directory allows traversal (entering it).

questions.question: What does SUID (Set User ID) do?
options: Runs the program with the file owner's permissions.
Grants group ownership to new files in a directory.
questions.explanation: SUID executes the file with the effective UID of the file owner.

questions.question: Which numeric `chmod` value should you use for a private SSH key (`id_rsa`)?
questions.explanation: 600 gives read/write to owner only. SSH refuses to use keys with group/other permissions.

questions.question: What is the correct way to edit the protected file `/etc/hosts`?
questions.explanation: You must use `sudo` to gain temporary root privileges to edit system files.

questions.question: To apply a permission change to a directory and all its contents, you use the `-_____` flag with `chmod` or `chown`.
questions.explanation: `-R` (uppercase) stands for Recursive.

questions.question: You forgot to type `sudo` before a long command. What shortcut reruns it with sudo?
questions.explanation: `!!` expands to the previous command.

questions.question: What is the numeric (octal) value of the Sticky Bit when used as a fourth digit?
questions.explanation: SUID=4, SGID=2, Sticky=1.

questions.question: Members of the `sudo` group can run any command as root without restriction.
questions.explanation: True, unless specifically restricted by the `/etc/sudoers` file. By default, the `sudo` group has full root access.

questions.question: Which command shows all groups your user belongs to?
questions.explanation: `groups` lists the groups for the current user.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `/etc/shadow` file?
questions.explanation: `/etc/shadow` stores hashed passwords and is readable only by root.

questions.question: You want to set exact permissions `rwxr-x---` using symbolic mode. Which command?
questions.explanation: All three commands achieve the same resulting permissions.

questions.question: Which flag makes `sudo` ask for the target user's password instead of the invoking user's?
options: This is not a feature of sudo.
questions.explanation: `sudo` always asks for the invoking user's password (or caches it). You cannot make it ask for root's password; that would be `su`.

questions.question: Why is being in the `docker` group a security risk?
options: Users can mount the host filesystem and effectively become root.
Docker containers use too much RAM.
The group name is easy to guess.
questions.explanation: Docker group membership allows mounting the host root filesystem into a container, giving the user full control over the host.
